books.title: Mind Without Mind
books.author: Marek Komar
books.summary: Mind without Mind is about unlocking your greatest asset - your mind.
books.notes:
- When we are in a state of flow, we are using less of our brain.
	- Inner critic/Idea of self goes away, perception of time changes.
	- Often an 'accident' when we enter the state of flow.
- Prerequisites for flow;
	- Focused attention
		- Don't control thoughts that enter our mind, but we control what thoughts we give attention to
			- Our overthinking self gets in the way of this often
			- Frustration at mistakes is just a distraction. Training to think of them this way we are more free of them.
	- Clear goals
		- **Setting clear micro steps to achieve our goal and bring attention to what needs to be done at the moment**
			- **Micro steps help us shift focus from rumination to thinking about how we are doing what we are doing (focused attention)**
				- EX) That lamp was bad -> what do I need to do to win this fight?
		- Clear objectives it clarifies our thoughts and actions
	- **Balance between skill and challenge**
		- One of the most important prerequisites for flow
		- 4% rule states that the challenge should be roughly 4% higher than our skill level
			- Don't take this to heart, more of a general rule than a strict one
	- Feedback
		- Should be immediate and clear
			- Shorter the gap between effort and feedback the better
		- Should be frequent
		- The more someone understands role and gets consistent feedback the better, group flow can occur the more people on a team feel this way
	- Environmental factors for flow
		- High risk/consequences
			- Think of high risk sports or activities like cliff jumping, how they are in flow because of the risk of the consequences
		- Rich/new environments
		- Pattern Recognition
			- Think of aim training, we are practicing and learning certain things that we can apply at a later time
				- EX) PGTI for falling targets, VoxTS for flicking
		- Deep embodiment/coordination of different senses
			- Understanding how you feel in different parts of your body and how they are coordinating together to accomplish your task
- "Discipline without surrender is just ego" not every game is winnable
	- "It is an unwinnable game that taunts you with the idea that doing more will bring you success."
	- Water doesn't try to go through its problems, it goes around, and becomes what it takes the shape of (water bottle, cup of water), try to be like water.
- The number of hours doesn't matter if your mind is not present during practice
	- Hours of conscious practice > hours of unconscious practice
- Mental Performance Training (MPT)
	- 3 parts of training;
		- Self Awareness
			- Feedback is crucial to improvement
			- Understand your personal philosophy and what your goal means to you
		- Clear Objectives
			- Set process oriented goals that you can control, since you cannot control the outcome of bigger, outcome oriented goals.
		- Mindfulness Training
			- Understand frustration are thoughts you don't need to give attention to.
			- We control what thoughts we give attention to, even though we don't control what thoughts come into our heads.
			- Single point mindfulness is when we have a negative thought, we acknowledge it, let it go, and focus on something else.
			- Instead of "Why am I stressed?", go "I am stressed". Live in the now, which you have control over.
	- We can only control our thoughts, attitude, and actions, so it's pointless to focus on things outside of our control for the sake of improvement.
	- Improving 1% a day over a 100 day block is a huge improvement
- Higher level performers mostly need to focus on getting out of their own way rather than learning new things.
- Meditation;
	- Meditation is all about focus.
	- Focus on your breathing for 10 counts, start at 1 if you get distracted, if its too easy then do it for 30 seconds.
- OODA; Observe, Orient, Decide, Act
- 3 As; Awareness, Acceptance, Action
	- Box Breathing - 4 in, hold for 4, out for 4, hold for 4

books.title: The Talent Code
books.author: Daniel Coyle
books.summary: The Talent Code is about physically growing your ultimate advantage - your own talent.

books.title: Legacy
books.author: James Kerr
books.summary: Legacy is about forging your most enduring competitive advantage - your character.
